news_summary: Recent scientific breakthroughs are advancing drug modification, uncovering links between DNA repair and serious diseases, enhancing disease testing with deep-sea proteins, and improving men's health through a helpful smartphone app.
news_summary_fr: Les récentes avancées scientifiques permettent de faire progresser la modification des médicaments, de mettre en lumière les liens entre la réparation de l'ADN et certaines maladies graves, d'améliorer les tests de dépistage grâce à des protéines issues des profondeurs marines et d'améliorer la santé masculine grâce à une application mobile utile.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances científicos están impulsando la modificación de fármacos, revelando vínculos entre la reparación del ADN y enfermedades graves, mejorando las pruebas de detección de enfermedades gracias a proteínas de las profundidades marinas y mejorando la salud masculina mediante una útil aplicación para teléfonos inteligentes.

articles.title: A lab mistake at Cambridge reveals a powerful new way to modify drug molecules
articles.summary: Cambridge scientists have discovered a light-powered chemical reaction that lets researchers modify complex drug molecules at the final stages of development. Unlike traditional methods that rely on toxic chemicals and harsh conditions, the new approach uses an LED lamp to create essential carbon–carbon bonds under mild conditions. This could make drug discovery faster and more environmentally friendly. The breakthrough was uncovered unexpectedly during a failed laboratory experiment.
articles.content:
Researchers at the University of Cambridge have created a new technique that uses light instead of toxic chemicals to change complex drug molecules. The discovery could speed up drug development and make the process of designing medicines more efficient.

The study, published on March 12 in Nature Synthesis, introduces what the team calls an "anti-Friedel-Crafts" reaction. Traditional Friedel-Crafts chemistry requires powerful chemicals or metal catalysts and harsh laboratory conditions. Because of these requirements, the reaction normally takes place early in drug manufacturing and is followed by many additional chemical steps to produce the final medicine.

The new Cambridge method turns that process around by allowing researchers to make changes to drug molecules much later in development.

LED Powered Reaction Forms Key Chemical Bonds

Instead of relying on heavy metal catalysts, the reaction is activated by an LED lamp at ambient temperature. When the light triggers the reaction, it sets off a self sustaining chain process that forms carbon-carbon bonds under mild conditions without toxic or costly reagents.

In practical terms, this approach lets chemists adjust complex molecules near the end of the drug development process rather than dismantling them and rebuilding them piece by piece -- something that can otherwise take months.

"We've found a new way to make precise changes to complex drug molecules, particularly ones that have been exceptionally difficult to modify in the past," said David Vahey, first author and a PhD researcher at St John's College, Cambridge.

"Scientists can spend months rebuilding large parts of a molecule just to test one small change. Now, instead of doing a multistep process for hundreds of molecules, scientists can start with their hit and make small modifications later on."

"This reaction lets scientists make precise adjustments much later in the process, under mild conditions and without relying on toxic or expensive reagents. That opens chemical space that has been hard to access before and gives medicinal chemists a cleaner, more efficient tool for exploring new versions of a drug."

Faster Drug Discovery With Less Waste

Reducing the number of synthesis steps lowers chemical use, cuts energy consumption and shrinks the environmental footprint of drug development. It also saves researchers valuable time.

The reaction is highly selective, allowing chemists to change one specific part of a molecule without disturbing other sensitive areas. This precision is important because even small structural changes can influence how a medicine works in the body, how it behaves biologically or whether it produces side effects.

At its core, the breakthrough addresses a fundamental chemical challenge: forming carbon-carbon bonds. These bonds create the backbone of countless substances including fuels, plastics and complex biological molecules.

The technique also shows what chemists describe as "high functional-group tolerance." That means it can modify one region of a molecule while leaving other functional groups untouched. This makes the reaction particularly useful for late-stage optimization, a stage of drug discovery where scientists fine tune molecules to improve how medicines perform.

Because the approach avoids heavy metals, harsh reaction conditions and lengthy synthesis pathways, it could also reduce toxic waste and energy consumption in pharmaceutical manufacturing. These environmental benefits are increasingly important as the chemical industry works to reduce its environmental impact.

Inspired by Sustainable Chemistry Research

Vahey works in the research group led by Professor Erwin Reisner at Cambridge. Reisner's team is known for developing chemical systems inspired by photosynthesis. Their research explores ways to use sunlight to convert waste materials, water and the greenhouse gas carbon dioxide into useful chemicals and fuels.

Reisner, Professor of Energy and Sustainability in the Yusuf Hamied Department of Chemistry and lead author of the study, said the significance of the work lies in expanding what chemists can achieve under practical conditions while also moving toward greener manufacturing techniques.

"This is a new way to make a fundamental carbon-carbon bond and that's why the potential impact is so great. It also means chemists can avoid an undesirable and inefficient drug modification process."

The researchers tested the reaction on a broad range of drug like molecules and showed that it could also be adapted for continuous flow systems commonly used in industrial chemical production. Collaboration with AstraZeneca helped evaluate whether the technique could meet the practical and environmental requirements of large scale pharmaceutical manufacturing.

"Transitioning the chemical industry to a sustainable industry is arguably one of the most difficult parts of the whole energy transition," explained Reisner.

Breakthrough Emerges From a Failed Experiment

The discovery began with an unexpected laboratory result, similar to many famous scientific breakthroughs including X-rays, penicillin, Viagra and modern weight loss medications.

"Failure after failure, then we found something we weren't expecting in the mess -- a real diamond in the rough. And it is all thanks to a failed control experiment," Vahey said.

He had been testing a photocatalyst when he removed it during a control experiment and discovered that the reaction worked just as well and sometimes even better without it.

At first the unusual product appeared to be a mistake. Instead of ignoring it, the researchers chose to investigate further. According to Reisner, recognizing the significance of unexpected results is an important part of scientific discovery.

"Recognizing the value in the unexpected is probably one of the key characteristics of a successful scientist," he said.

AI Helps Predict New Chemical Reactions

"We generate enormous amounts of data, and increasingly we use artificial intelligence to help analyze it. We have an algorithm that can predict reactivity. AI helps because we don't need chemists to do endless trial and error, but an algorithm will only follow the rules it has been given. It still takes a human being to look at something that appears wrong and ask whether it might actually be something new."

In this case, Vahey recognized the potential importance of the unexpected result and explored it further.

"David could have dismissed it as a failed control," Reisner said. "Instead, he stopped and thought about what he was seeing. That moment, choosing to investigate rather than ignore it, is where discovery happens."

After uncovering the chemistry behind the reaction, the team introduced machine learning models developed with Trinity College Dublin to predict where the reaction would occur on entirely new molecules that had never been tested in the laboratory.

By learning patterns from known chemical reactions, the AI system can simulate possible outcomes before experiments are performed. This allows researchers to identify promising molecules more quickly and with far less trial and error.

For Vahey, the discovery provides scientists with a valuable new capability for drug discovery and development.

He said: "What industry and other researchers do with it next -- that's where the future impact lies. For us, the lab is mostly average to bad days. The good days are very good days."

Reisner added: "As a chemist, you only need one or two good days a year -- and those can come from a failed experiment."

10 Famous Accidental Scientific Discoveries 1. X-rays (1895)

Wilhelm Conrad Röntgen discovered X-rays while studying electrical currents flowing through glass tubes. He noticed that a nearby screen began glowing unexpectedly, revealing a new type of radiation that allowed doctors to see inside the human body without surgery.

2. Radioactivity (1898)

Marie Curie observed that certain uranium minerals produced much more radiation than uranium alone could explain. This surprising finding led to the discovery of polonium and radium and helped establish the field of nuclear physics and chemistry.

3. Vulcanized rubber (1839)

Charles Goodyear discovered vulcanization when a mixture of natural rubber and sulphur accidentally fell onto a hot surface. Instead of melting, the rubber became strong and elastic. The process made rubber practical for industrial uses and eventually enabled the development of tires and many other products.

4. Penicillin (1928)

Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin after mould accidentally contaminated a laboratory dish and killed surrounding bacteria. The discovery led to the first widely used antibiotic and transformed modern medicine.

5. Teflon (1938)

Chemist Roy Plunkett accidentally created Teflon while experimenting with refrigerant gases. The unexpected material proved extremely slippery and heat resistant and later became widely used in nonstick cookware and industrial applications.

6. Super glue (1942)

Harry Coover was attempting to develop transparent plastics when he instead created a substance that bonded instantly to nearly any surface. Later marketed as super glue, it became widely used in homes, manufacturing and medicine.

7. LSD (1943)

Swiss chemist Albert Hofmann accidentally absorbed a small amount of a compound he had synthesized and experienced its powerful psychological effects. The substance, lysergic acid diethylamide (LSD), later played an important role in neuroscience research and became controversial in popular culture.

8. Pulsars (1967)

Graduate student Jocelyn Bell Burnell noticed repeating radio signals while analyzing telescope data. Initially believed to be interference, the signals turned out to be the first evidence of pulsars, rapidly spinning neutron stars that opened a new field of astrophysics.

9. Viagra (1990s)

Researchers at Pfizer were studying a drug intended to treat angina when participants reported an unexpected side effect. The compound was later developed as Viagra and is now widely prescribed for erectile dysfunction.

10. Weight loss injections (2021)

Scientists developing treatments for Type 2 diabetes discovered that drugs mimicking the hormone GLP-1 also caused significant weight loss. Medications such as Ozempic and Mounjaro, originally created for diabetes, were later developed to treat obesity, marking a major shift in approaches to weight management.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant scientific breakthrough in drug development chemistry that enables faster, cleaner, and more efficient modification of complex drug molecules using a light-powered reaction. This innovation has broad implications for accelerating drug discovery, reducing toxic chemical use, lowering environmental impact, and improving pharmaceutical manufacturing processes. The story is well-detailed, focused on a single major advancement with clear real-world benefits for society and the environment.
articles.personality_title: Cambridge scientists find a cleaner, faster way to improve drug molecules using light
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Developing new medicines often requires changing complex molecules using harsh chemicals and many steps. This process can be slow, costly, and produce toxic waste.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Cambridge discovered a new chemical reaction powered by LED light that allows them to modify drug molecules at the final stages of development. This method creates important carbon–carbon bonds without using toxic metals or harsh conditions. The discovery came from a surprising result during an experiment that did not go as planned.

**Impact** – This new light-driven reaction lets scientists adjust complex drugs more easily and precisely, saving time and reducing harmful chemicals. It can change one part of a molecule without damaging others, which is important for making medicines work better and safer. Because it works under mild conditions and avoids toxic waste, it could make drug development faster and more environmentally friendly.

**What's next step** – The team will continue testing this reaction on different drug molecules and explore how to use it in large-scale pharmaceutical manufacturing. They also use artificial intelligence to predict how the reaction will work on new molecules, speeding up research and reducing trial and error.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Cambridge scientists have created a light-powered chemical method that makes improving complex drug molecules faster, cleaner, and more precise than before.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques de Cambridge découvrent une méthode plus propre et plus rapide pour améliorer les médicaments grâce à la lumière
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le développement de nouveaux médicaments nécessite souvent de modifier des molécules complexes avec des produits chimiques agressifs et de nombreuses étapes. Ce processus peut être lent, coûteux et produire des déchets toxiques.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Cambridge ont découvert une nouvelle réaction chimique activée par une lampe LED qui permet de modifier les molécules de médicaments à un stade avancé du développement. Cette méthode crée des liaisons carbone-carbone importantes sans utiliser de métaux toxiques ni de conditions sévères. La découverte est venue d’un résultat surprenant lors d’une expérience ratée.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle réaction alimentée par la lumière permet aux scientifiques d’ajuster les médicaments complexes plus facilement et précisément, en gagnant du temps et en réduisant l’utilisation de produits chimiques nocifs. Elle peut modifier une partie d’une molécule sans abîmer les autres, ce qui est important pour améliorer l’efficacité et la sécurité des médicaments. Fonctionnant dans des conditions douces et évitant les déchets toxiques, elle pourrait rendre le développement des médicaments plus rapide et plus respectueux de l’environnement.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe va continuer à tester cette réaction sur différentes molécules médicamenteuses et étudier son utilisation à grande échelle dans l’industrie pharmaceutique. Ils utilisent aussi l’intelligence artificielle pour prédire le fonctionnement de la réaction sur de nouvelles molécules, accélérant la recherche et réduisant les essais inutiles.

**Phrase clé** – Des scientifiques de Cambridge ont créé une méthode chimique activée par la lumière qui rend l’amélioration des molécules complexes plus rapide, plus propre et plus précise qu’avant.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos de Cambridge encuentran una forma más limpia y rápida de mejorar moléculas de medicamentos usando luz
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Desarrollar nuevos medicamentos a menudo requiere cambiar moléculas complejas usando productos químicos fuertes y muchos pasos. Este proceso puede ser lento, costoso y producir residuos tóxicos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Cambridge descubrieron una nueva reacción química activada por luz LED que les permite modificar moléculas de medicamentos en las etapas finales de desarrollo. Este método crea enlaces carbono-carbono importantes sin usar metales tóxicos ni condiciones duras. El descubrimiento surgió de un resultado inesperado durante un experimento fallido.

**Impacto** – Esta reacción impulsada por luz permite a los científicos ajustar medicamentos complejos con más facilidad y precisión, ahorrando tiempo y reduciendo químicos dañinos. Puede cambiar una parte de la molécula sin dañar otras, lo cual es clave para mejorar cómo funcionan y su seguridad. Al funcionar en condiciones suaves y evitar residuos tóxicos, podría hacer el desarrollo de medicamentos más rápido y amigable con el medio ambiente.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo seguirá probando esta reacción en diferentes moléculas medicinales y explorará su uso en producción farmacéutica a gran escala. También usan inteligencia artificial para predecir cómo funcionará la reacción en nuevas moléculas, acelerando la investigación y reduciendo pruebas innecesarias.

**Frase clave** – Científicos de Cambridge han creado un método químico activado por luz que hace que mejorar moléculas complejas de medicamentos sea más rápido, limpio y preciso que antes.
articles.image_prompt: A softly glowing LED lamp illuminating a delicate, intricate molecular structure made of interlocking carbon rings and bonds, set against a warm, muted background symbolizing a laboratory; nearby, a subtle, abstract sparkle or diamond shape represents the unexpected discovery and breakthrough in green chemistry.

articles.title: Scientists discover ALS protein that links DNA repair to cancer and dementia
articles.summary: A protein tied to ALS and dementia may have a much bigger role in disease than scientists realized. Researchers found that TDP43 controls a key DNA repair process, but when the protein becomes imbalanced, the repair system can spiral out of control, harming neurons and destabilizing DNA. The team also discovered that high levels of the protein are linked to increased mutation rates in cancer. The discovery places TDP43 at the center of both neurodegeneration and cancer biology.
articles.content:
Researchers at Houston Methodist have discovered that a protein tied to neurodegenerative conditions such as dementia and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) also helps control a critical DNA repair process. This repair system, known as DNA mismatch repair, corrects mistakes that occur when cells copy genetic material. The discovery suggests that the protein may influence both brain diseases and cancer, potentially reshaping how scientists think about these major health conditions.

The study, published in Nucleic Acids Research, shows that the protein 'TDP43' regulates genes responsible for fixing DNA errors. When levels of this protein drop too low or rise too high, those repair genes become overly active. Instead of protecting cells, the heightened repair activity can harm neurons and destabilize the genome, which may increase the risk of cancer.

TDP43 Plays a Critical Role in DNA Mismatch Repair

"DNA repair is one of the most fundamental processes in biology," said lead investigator Muralidhar L. Hegde, Ph.D., professor of neurosurgery at the Houston Methodist Research Institute's Center for Neuroregeneration. "What we found is that TDP43 is not just another RNA-binding protein involved in splicing, but a critical regulator of mismatch repair machinery. That has major implications for diseases like ALS and frontotemporal dementia (FTD) where this protein goes awry."

The researchers also uncovered evidence linking the protein to cancer. By analyzing large cancer databases, the team found that higher amounts of TDP43 were associated with greater numbers of mutations in tumors.

Protein Links Neurodegeneration and Cancer

"This tells us that the biology of this protein is broader than just ALS or FTD," Hegde said. "In cancers, this protein appears to be upregulated and linked to increased mutation load. That puts it at the intersection of two of the most important disease categories of our time: neurodegeneration and cancer."

The scientists say the findings may also point toward new treatment approaches. In laboratory models, reducing the excessive DNA repair activity caused by abnormal TDP43 helped partially reverse cellular damage. Hegde said that Controlling DNA mismatch repair may offer a therapeutic strategy.

Other collaborators in the study were Vincent Provasek, Suganya Rangaswamy, Manohar Kodavati, Joy Mitra, Vikas Malojirao, Velmarini Vasquez, Gavin Britz and Sankar Mitra from Houston Methodist; Albino Bacolla and John Tainer from MD Anderson Cancer Center; Issa Yusuf and Zuoshang Xu from University of Massachusetts; Guo-Min Li from UT Southwestern Medical Center and Ralph Garruto from Binghamton University.

The research was primarily supported by the National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke (NINDS) and the National Institute on Aging of the National Institutes of Health (NIH), the Sherman Foundation Parkinson's Disease Research Challenge Fund and internal funding from the Houston Methodist Research Institute.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery linking a protein involved in ALS and dementia to DNA repair and cancer mutation rates, offering new insights that could lead to novel treatment strategies for major diseases affecting many people worldwide.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find protein that links brain diseases and cancer through DNA repair
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long studied a protein called TDP43 because it is connected to brain diseases like amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) and dementia. These diseases cause nerve cells to stop working properly. DNA repair, a process that fixes mistakes in our genetic code, is essential for keeping cells healthy.

**What happened** – Researchers at Houston Methodist discovered that TDP43 helps control a key DNA repair system called mismatch repair. When TDP43 levels are too high or too low, this repair process becomes unbalanced. This can damage nerve cells and make DNA unstable. The scientists also found that high amounts of TDP43 are linked to more mutations in cancer cells.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that TDP43 is important not only in brain diseases but also in cancer. It helps explain why problems with DNA repair can lead to both nerve damage and cancer growth. Understanding TDP43’s role could change how doctors think about these diseases and point to new ways to treat them.

**What's next step** – The researchers plan to explore treatments that control the DNA repair activity affected by TDP43. In lab tests, adjusting this repair process helped reduce cell damage. This could lead to new medicines that protect nerve cells or slow cancer by fixing how DNA is repaired.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The protein TDP43 connects brain diseases and cancer through its control of DNA repair, offering a new target for future treatments.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent une protéine qui relie maladies cérébrales et cancer via la réparation de l’ADN
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient depuis longtemps une protéine appelée TDP43, liée à des maladies cérébrales comme la sclérose latérale amyotrophique (SLA) et la démence. Ces maladies empêchent les cellules nerveuses de fonctionner correctement. La réparation de l’ADN est un processus essentiel qui corrige les erreurs dans notre code génétique.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de Houston Methodist ont découvert que TDP43 contrôle un système clé de réparation de l’ADN appelé réparation des mésappariements. Lorsque le niveau de TDP43 est trop élevé ou trop bas, ce processus de réparation devient déséquilibré. Cela peut endommager les cellules nerveuses et rendre l’ADN instable. Les scientifiques ont aussi trouvé que de fortes quantités de TDP43 sont liées à plus de mutations dans les cellules cancéreuses.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que TDP43 est important non seulement dans les maladies cérébrales, mais aussi dans le cancer. Elle explique pourquoi des problèmes de réparation de l’ADN peuvent causer à la fois des dégâts aux nerfs et la croissance du cancer. Comprendre le rôle de TDP43 pourrait changer la façon dont les médecins abordent ces maladies et ouvrir la voie à de nouveaux traitements.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent tester des traitements qui régulent l’activité de réparation de l’ADN contrôlée par TDP43. Dans des tests en laboratoire, ajuster ce processus a aidé à réduire les dommages cellulaires. Cela pourrait conduire à de nouveaux médicaments pour protéger les cellules nerveuses ou ralentir le cancer.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La protéine TDP43 relie maladies cérébrales et cancer par son contrôle de la réparation de l’ADN, offrant une nouvelle cible pour les traitements futurs.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren proteína que conecta enfermedades cerebrales y cáncer a través de la reparación del ADN
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estudiado durante mucho tiempo una proteína llamada TDP43 porque está relacionada con enfermedades cerebrales como la esclerosis lateral amiotrófica (ELA) y la demencia. Estas enfermedades hacen que las células nerviosas dejen de funcionar bien. La reparación del ADN es un proceso importante que corrige errores en nuestro código genético.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de Houston Methodist descubrieron que TDP43 controla un sistema clave de reparación del ADN llamado reparación de desajustes. Cuando los niveles de TDP43 son demasiado altos o bajos, este proceso se desequilibra. Esto puede dañar las células nerviosas y hacer que el ADN sea inestable. También encontraron que altos niveles de TDP43 están relacionados con más mutaciones en las células cancerosas.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra que TDP43 es importante no solo en enfermedades cerebrales, sino también en el cáncer. Ayuda a explicar por qué los problemas en la reparación del ADN pueden causar daño a los nervios y crecimiento del cáncer. Entender el papel de TDP43 podría cambiar la forma en que los médicos piensan sobre estas enfermedades y abrir nuevas vías para tratarlas.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean explorar tratamientos que controlen la actividad de reparación del ADN afectada por TDP43. En pruebas de laboratorio, ajustar este proceso ayudó a reducir el daño celular. Esto podría llevar a nuevos medicamentos que protejan las células nerviosas o frenen el cáncer.

**Frase clave** – La proteína TDP43 conecta enfermedades cerebrales y cáncer a través de su control sobre la reparación del ADN, ofreciendo un nuevo objetivo para futuros tratamientos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of an intricate double helix DNA strand gently intertwined with glowing, thread-like protein structures labeled by subtle abstract shapes representing TDP43, surrounded by softly illuminated neuron silhouettes and delicate cellular repair tools symbolizing DNA mismatch repair, all set against a calm, neutral background with natural, muted colors.

articles.title: Hidden deep-sea proteins could supercharge disease tests
articles.summary: Hidden in volcanic lakes and deep-sea vents, scientists have discovered tough new DNA-binding proteins built to survive extreme conditions. After scanning huge genetic databases, researchers found molecules that remain stable under heat, salt, and harsh chemistry. One of these proteins significantly improved rapid LAMP diagnostic tests, making them faster and more sensitive. The discovery could help create better tools for detecting infectious diseases.
articles.content:
Researchers have identified previously unknown DNA-binding proteins in some of the most extreme places on Earth and demonstrated that they can enhance rapid medical tests used to detect infectious diseases.

The international team was led by Durham University and included collaborators in Iceland, Norway, and Poland. The scientists examined genetic material collected from volcanic lakes in Iceland and from deep-sea hydrothermal vents located more than two kilometers beneath the North Atlantic Ocean.

Mining Nature's Genetic Diversity

Nature is one of the richest sources of useful enzymes, yet many remain undiscovered. To uncover new candidates, the researchers used next-generation DNA sequencing to scan massive databases containing millions of potential protein sequences.

By analyzing this vast amount of genetic information, the team identified previously unknown proteins that attach to single-stranded DNA. These proteins also showed an unusual ability to remain stable under extreme conditions, including very high temperatures, extreme pH levels, and highly salty environments.

Robust Proteins With Biotech Potential

The newly identified proteins were examined in detail using a range of laboratory methods. The scientists found that these molecules are exceptionally durable, with strong thermal stability that makes them well suited for applications in biotechnology and medicine.

Researchers also determined the proteins' three-dimensional structures at high resolution. This structural information provides insight into how the molecules function and opens the door to refining them through protein design.

Improving Rapid Diagnostic Tests

One of the newly discovered DNA-binding proteins was found to enhance diagnostic tests that use loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP). These tests detect genetic material from viruses, bacteria, or parasites without requiring complex laboratory equipment.

When the new protein was added, the LAMP tests became both faster and more sensitive. This improvement helped increase the detection of viral RNA from pathogens such as SARS-CoV-2, as well as DNA from other infectious agents.

The results demonstrate the potential of extreme environments as a source of valuable biological tools.

Lead investigator of the study, Professor Ehmke Pohl of Durham University, said: "This work highlights the enormous potential of bioprospecting from extreme habitats. The results are not only important for the bioeconomy, but they also provide the basis for all Artificial Intelligence (AI) methods in protein structure prediction and protein design."

Applications in Biotechnology and AI-Driven Protein Design

Biotechnology companies are continually searching for enzymes that can perform reliably under demanding conditions. Proteins found in places such as hot springs or deep ocean vents are particularly promising because they naturally function in harsh environments.

These discoveries may also contribute to broader research in protein prediction and design. Artificial intelligence systems that model protein structures benefit from large and diverse sets of real biological examples.

Searching for More Proteins and New Diagnostics

The researchers are continuing to investigate additional DNA-binding proteins, and several promising candidates have already been identified. Scientists are also developing improved versions of the proteins and designing new LAMP tests aimed at neglected tropical diseases, including leishmaniasis and Chagas disease. This work is being carried out in collaboration with researchers in Durham University's Biosciences Department.

The team is also working with the Norwegian biotechnology company ArcticZymes to explore potential commercial uses for the newly discovered proteins.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of robust DNA-binding proteins from extreme environments that improve rapid diagnostic tests for infectious diseases, enhancing speed and sensitivity. This breakthrough has broad implications for public health, especially in detecting diseases more effectively and developing new diagnostics for neglected tropical diseases. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights both the scientific achievement and its real-world application.
articles.personality_title: New deep-sea proteins improve quick disease tests
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists searched extreme places like volcanic lakes and deep ocean vents to find new proteins. These places have harsh conditions such as high heat and salt, where most proteins would break down.

**What happened** – An international research team led by Durham University discovered previously unknown DNA-binding proteins that stay stable in these extreme environments. They found these proteins by scanning large genetic databases. One protein was tested and made rapid disease tests, called LAMP tests, faster and better at detecting viruses and bacteria.

**Impact** – These proteins are special because they work well under tough conditions, making them useful for medical tests that don’t need complicated machines. This could help detect diseases like COVID-19 more quickly and accurately. The discovery also helps scientists design new proteins using artificial intelligence.

**What's next step** – Researchers are studying more proteins from these environments and improving the ones they found. They plan to create better tests for neglected diseases like leishmaniasis and Chagas disease. They are also working with companies to develop commercial uses for these proteins.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists found strong proteins from extreme places that make quick disease tests faster and more sensitive, opening new paths for medical diagnostics.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouvelles protéines des fonds marins améliorent les tests rapides de maladies
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Des scientifiques ont exploré des endroits extrêmes comme des lacs volcaniques et des sources chaudes sous-marines où les conditions sont très difficiles, avec beaucoup de chaleur et de sel.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe internationale dirigée par l’université de Durham a découvert des protéines inconnues qui se fixent à l’ADN et restent stables dans ces milieux extrêmes. Ils ont trouvé ces protéines en analysant de grandes bases de données génétiques. L’une d’elles a amélioré des tests rapides appelés tests LAMP, les rendant plus rapides et plus précis pour détecter virus et bactéries.

**Impact** – Ces protéines sont spéciales car elles fonctionnent bien dans des conditions difficiles, ce qui est utile pour des tests médicaux simples et rapides. Cela pourrait aider à détecter plus vite des maladies comme la COVID-19. Cette découverte aide aussi à concevoir de nouvelles protéines grâce à l’intelligence artificielle.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs étudient d’autres protéines et améliorent celles qu’ils ont trouvées. Ils veulent créer de meilleurs tests pour des maladies négligées comme la leishmaniose et la maladie de Chagas. Ils collaborent aussi avec des entreprises pour utiliser ces protéines commercialement.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des protéines solides venues de milieux extrêmes rendent les tests rapides de maladies plus rapides et sensibles, ouvrant de nouvelles possibilités pour le diagnostic médical.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevas proteínas de aguas profundas mejoran pruebas rápidas de enfermedades
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Científicos buscaron proteínas en lugares extremos como lagos volcánicos y respiraderos marinos profundos, donde las condiciones son muy duras, con mucho calor y sal.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo internacional liderado por la Universidad de Durham descubrió proteínas desconocidas que se unen al ADN y permanecen estables en estos ambientes extremos. Encontraron estas proteínas analizando grandes bases de datos genéticas. Una proteína mejoró pruebas rápidas llamadas pruebas LAMP, haciéndolas más rápidas y sensibles para detectar virus y bacterias.

**Impacto** – Estas proteínas son especiales porque funcionan bien en condiciones difíciles, lo que es útil para pruebas médicas simples y rápidas. Esto podría ayudar a detectar enfermedades como COVID-19 más rápido y con mayor precisión. El descubrimiento también ayuda a diseñar nuevas proteínas usando inteligencia artificial.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores están estudiando más proteínas y mejorando las que encontraron. Quieren crear mejores pruebas para enfermedades desatendidas como leishmaniasis y enfermedad de Chagas. También trabajan con empresas para desarrollar usos comerciales de estas proteínas.

**Frase clave** – Científicos encontraron proteínas fuertes de lugares extremos que hacen que las pruebas rápidas de enfermedades sean más rápidas y sensibles, abriendo nuevas posibilidades para diagnósticos médicos.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate underwater scene showing glowing, stylized DNA strands entwined with robust, crystalline protein structures emerging from a deep-sea hydrothermal vent, illuminated by warm golden light filtering through dark ocean depths, symbolizing discovery and scientific breakthrough.

articles.title: A smartphone app can help men last longer in bed
articles.summary: In a randomised trial, men who experience premature ejaculation benefitted from using an app to learn techniques for extending intercourse
articles.content:
In a randomised trial, men who experience premature ejaculation benefitted from using an app to learn techniques for extending intercourse

Premature ejaculation is thought to affect 1 in 3 men Ievgen Chabanov / Alamy

Men who experience premature ejaculation may be able to improve control and extend intercourse using a smartphone app, according to preliminary results from a small randomised trial.

Premature ejaculation is considered the most common sexual dysfunction among men, estimated to affect around 1 in 3 men. An array of treatment options including drugs such as topical anaesthetics or selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) can delay ejaculation but must be taken repeatedly and may cause side effects. The pills also have to be taken some time before sex, which takes away from the spontaneity of the act. “The existing medical treatments are not curing the situation,” says Christer Groeben at Heidelberg University in Germany.

Advertisement

The app, Melonga, offers men a curriculum designed by psychologists and urologists that combines arousal-awareness training, pelvic floor exercises, mindfulness techniques and cognitive behavioural strategies. Users are taught to recognise the “point of no return” before ejaculation and use breathing, relaxation and start-stop techniques to reduce arousal. Modules also encourage communication with partners and challenge negative thought patterns through cognitive behavioural therapy.

In the trial, 80 men were randomised to either use the app or receive no structured intervention over an initial 12 weeks. Among the 66 participants who completed the study, those using the app increased their intravaginal ejaculation latency time from an average of 61 seconds to 125 seconds, with results seen after four weeks of use. The control group saw virtually no change.

Men who used the app reported that the benefits extended to a reduced impact on their relationship and improvement in the enjoyment of sex, tied to its longer duration. Premature ejaculation can be caused by prostate or thyroid issues, while it can also overlap with depression, but the inclusion criteria for the study ensured only healthy participants were selected.

Free newsletter Sign up to Eight Weeks to a Healthier You Your science-backed guide to the easy habits that will help you sleep well, stress less, eat smarter and age better. Sign up to newsletter

Groeben presented the findings at the European Association of Urology congress in London today.

“Doctors prescribe pills much more than simple solutions,” says Giorgio Russo at the University of Catania, Italy, who was not involved in the research. “In 10 or 15 minutes [for an appointment], pills are the easiest solution for the doctor, but not for the patient.” The app, he says, “is like having a doctor on your phone” – giving men and their partners the opportunity to privately “understand their premature ejaculation issue”.

Russo describes the effects of the app, which was developed by Netherlands-based health start-up Prognoix, as “dramatic” – with 22 per cent of participants no longer qualifying for the definition of premature ejaculation after using it. “Improving just 1 or 2 minutes is a big result,” he says.

“One of the reasons patients have premature ejaculation is anxiety,” Russo adds. “Kegel [pelvic floor] exercises and muscle control can help them manage that anxiety and control the reflex.”

A slew of similar apps to combat premature ejaculation already exist, but none have been tested in a controlled study. One advantage of a digital approach is discretion. “A lot of patients don’t go to see their doctor about this condition because they feel a stigmatising effect when they sit in the waiting room with other patients,” says Groeben.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a randomized trial showing that a smartphone app can effectively help men with premature ejaculation, a common sexual dysfunction, improve control and extend intercourse duration. This represents a significant health breakthrough with broad potential impact, providing a non-pharmaceutical, accessible, and private treatment option that can improve quality of life and relationships. The study details and expert commentary provide sufficient substance and context.
articles.personality_title: Smartphone app helps men improve control over premature ejaculation
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Premature ejaculation is a common problem affecting about one in three men. It causes men to ejaculate sooner than they or their partners would like during sex. Usual treatments include medicines, but these can have side effects and need to be taken before sex.

**What happened** – A small study tested a smartphone app called Melonga, designed by doctors and psychologists. The app teaches men exercises and techniques to recognize when ejaculation is about to happen and how to delay it. In the study, 80 men were split into two groups: one used the app for 12 weeks, the other did not. After the study, men using the app doubled the time before ejaculation from about 1 minute to over 2 minutes.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows a new way to treat premature ejaculation without drugs. The app helps men privately learn to control their bodies and reduce anxiety, improving their sexual experience and relationships. Unlike pills, the app can be used anytime and keeps the treatment private, which many men prefer.

**What's next step** – Researchers will likely run larger studies to confirm these results and improve the app. If successful, this app or others like it could become a common, easy-to-use option for men facing this problem.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new smartphone app offers men a private, drug-free way to better control premature ejaculation and improve their sexual health.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une application smartphone aide les hommes à mieux contrôler l’éjaculation précoce
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’éjaculation précoce est un problème fréquent qui touche environ un homme sur trois. Elle conduit à une éjaculation plus rapide que souhaitée pendant les rapports sexuels. Les traitements habituels utilisent des médicaments, mais ceux-ci peuvent avoir des effets secondaires et doivent être pris avant le rapport.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une petite étude a testé une application smartphone appelée Melonga, conçue par des médecins et psychologues. L’application apprend aux hommes des exercices et techniques pour reconnaître le moment juste avant l’éjaculation et comment la retarder. Dans l’étude, 80 hommes ont été divisés en deux groupes : l’un a utilisé l’application pendant 12 semaines, l’autre non. Après l’étude, les hommes utilisant l’application ont doublé leur temps avant éjaculation, passant d’environ 1 minute à plus de 2 minutes.

**Impact** – Cela est important car cela montre une nouvelle façon de traiter l’éjaculation précoce sans médicaments. L’application aide les hommes à mieux contrôler leur corps et à réduire l’anxiété, améliorant ainsi leur expérience sexuelle et leurs relations. Contrairement aux pilules, l’application peut être utilisée à tout moment et offre un traitement discret, ce que beaucoup d’hommes préfèrent.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont probablement réaliser des études plus larges pour confirmer ces résultats et améliorer l’application. Si cela réussit, cette application ou d’autres similaires pourraient devenir une solution simple et courante pour les hommes concernés.

**En une phrase** – Une nouvelle application smartphone offre aux hommes un moyen discret et sans médicament de mieux contrôler l’éjaculation précoce et d’améliorer leur santé sexuelle.
articles.personality_title_es: Una app para smartphone ayuda a los hombres a controlar mejor la eyaculación precoz
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La eyaculación precoz es un problema común que afecta a aproximadamente uno de cada tres hombres. Hace que los hombres eyaculen antes de lo que desean durante las relaciones sexuales. Los tratamientos comunes incluyen medicamentos, pero estos pueden tener efectos secundarios y deben tomarse antes del sexo.

**Qué pasó** – Un pequeño estudio probó una aplicación para smartphone llamada Melonga, creada por médicos y psicólogos. La app enseña ejercicios y técnicas para que los hombres reconozcan el momento justo antes de eyacular y aprendan a retrasarlo. En el estudio, 80 hombres se dividieron en dos grupos: uno usó la app durante 12 semanas y el otro no. Al final, los hombres que usaron la app duplicaron el tiempo antes de eyacular, pasando de aproximadamente 1 minuto a más de 2 minutos.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra una nueva forma de tratar la eyaculación precoz sin medicamentos. La app ayuda a los hombres a controlar mejor su cuerpo y reducir la ansiedad, mejorando su experiencia sexual y sus relaciones. A diferencia de las pastillas, la app se puede usar en cualquier momento y ofrece privacidad, algo que muchos hombres prefieren.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probablemente harán estudios más grandes para confirmar estos resultados y mejorar la app. Si tiene éxito, esta app u otras similares podrían convertirse en una opción común y fácil para los hombres con este problema.

**Una frase clave** – Una nueva aplicación para smartphone ofrece a los hombres una forma privada y sin medicamentos para controlar mejor la eyaculación precoz y mejorar su salud sexual.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing smartphone displaying a gentle spiral of calming waves and rhythmic pulses, surrounded by soft, intertwined ribbons symbolizing connection and control, set against a soothing, natural-toned background that evokes mindfulness and gradual progress.
